alerts.alert_summary: Large-scale credential stuffing campaign from 17 distributed source IPs targeting 22+ distinct user accounts with over 28,000 total authentication failures across multiple waves spanning Jan 6–11, 2026.

reasons.challenge_id: The CREDENTIAL_STUFFING rule fired explicitly for all 17 source IPs, and the CREDENTIAL_STUFFING_USER_TARGETED rule fired for 29 distinct accounts — classic multi-source multi-account spray pattern consistent with credential stuffing.
reasons.severity: High severity: massive scale (28k+ failures), privileged accounts targeted (admin, sysadmin, root, administrator, devops), distributed attack infrastructure (17 IPs including external 185.220.101.x known Tor exits), and multi-day persistence indicating determined adversary.
reasons.alert_summary: Summarized the core metrics: IP count, account count, total failures, and time span from the aggregated CREDENTIAL_STUFFING rules.
reasons.remediation_proposal: Remediation targets immediate containment (IP blocks, account lockouts), hardening (MFA, rate limiting), and investigation (success checks, lateral movement hunting).

detection.attack_type: CREDENTIAL_STUFFING rule explicitly fired multiple times with high distinct_usernames counts (18-22 per source). The pattern of many IPs each trying many accounts is definitionally credential stuffing, not focused brute force on a single account.
attacker_ips: 10.0.0.1: 1831 failures against 18 accounts per CREDENTIAL_STUFFING rule
victim_accounts: sysadmin: 1790 failures, highest-value privileged account targeted
root: 571 failures in wave 2, superuser account
administrator: 180 failures in wave 2, privileged account
guest: 142 failures in wave 2, default account
test: 191 failures in wave 2, default account
oracle: 500 failures in wave 3, service account
postgres: 442 failures in wave 3, service account
deploy: 462 failures in wave 3, service account
centos: 468 failures in wave 3, OS default account
ubuntu: 441 failures in wave 3, OS default account
ec2-user: 466 failures in wave 3, cloud default account
debian: 497 failures in wave 3, OS default account
detection.attack_start_time: Earliest CREDENTIAL_STUFFING rule firing at 2026-01-06T00:00:43.693Z from 10.0.0.1
detection.attack_end_time: Latest CREDENTIAL_STUFFING_USER_TARGETED firing at 2026-01-11T01:00:03.932Z for debian account
detection.indicators: Aggregated from CREDENTIAL_STUFFING and CREDENTIAL_STUFFING_USER_TARGETED rules; total failures summed across all source IPs; distinct usernames from user-targeted rules; wave structure inferred from temporal gaps.

alerts.exhaustive_analysis:
A large-scale, distributed credential stuffing campaign was detected spanning from 2026-01-06 00:00 UTC to 2026-01-11 01:00 UTC. The attack occurred in three distinct waves:

Wave 1 (Jan 6, 00:00–01:01 UTC): 13 source IPs (mix of internal 10.x, 172.16.x, 192.168.x ranges and external 185.220.101.1/2) each generated ~1700-1846 authentication failures against 18 distinct user accounts. Targeted accounts include privileged ones (sysadmin with 1790 failures, admin with 1706 failures, devops with 1282 failures) and regular user accounts (nrobert, jdupont, fthomas, etc. each receiving 1200-1409 failures). The uniform distribution of 18 distinct usernames per source strongly suggests a shared credential list being rotated across a botnet.

Wave 2 (Jan 6, 03:02 UTC): Two external IPs (203.0.113.45 with 2540 failures, 198.51.100.23 with 1054 failures) targeted 22 distinct accounts including root (571 failures), administrator (180), guest (142), and test (191). This wave shifted focus to default/generic accounts.

Wave 3 (Jan 11, 01:00 UTC): Two IPs (198.51.100.89 with 1836 failures, 45.33.32.156 with 2814 failures) targeted 10 service/OS-default accounts (oracle, postgres, deploy, centos, ubuntu, ec2-user, debian). These IPs also triggered SSH_BRUTEFORCE_SSH_ONLY and SSH_BRUTEFORCE_SUSPICIOUS_GEO rules, showing all attempts used SSH protocol and geolocated to Russia (RU) against a baseline of France (FR).

The use of internal IP ranges (10.x, 172.16.x, 192.168.x) in Wave 1 suggests either compromised internal hosts acting as proxies/bots, or the attacker has positioned infrastructure inside the network. The 185.220.101.x IPs are known Tor exit nodes. The coordinated timing, uniform credential lists, and distributed sources are hallmarks of an organized credential stuffing operation likely using leaked credential databases.
alerts.remediation_proposal:
1) IMMEDIATE: Block all external attacker IPs at perimeter firewall: 185.220.101.1, 185.220.101.2, 203.0.113.45, 198.51.100.23, 198.51.100.89, 45.33.32.156. Block Tor exit node ranges if policy permits.
2) INVESTIGATE INTERNAL IPs: 10.0.0.1-4, 172.16.0.10-12, 192.168.1.100-103 are internal — investigate for compromise; isolate from network pending analysis; check for malware, unauthorized SSH tunnels, or proxy services.
3) ACCOUNT SECURITY: Force password resets for all 29 targeted accounts, prioritizing sysadmin, admin, root, administrator, devops. Lock accounts pending reset confirmation. Enable MFA on all accounts immediately.
4) RATE LIMITING: Deploy progressive authentication delays and account lockout after 5 failures; implement CAPTCHA on web auth endpoints.
5) GEO-BLOCKING: Block SSH from RU geolocation given FR baseline; restrict SSH access to known admin IP ranges via firewall/ACL.
6) DETECTION TUNING: Lower credential stuffing detection thresholds; add correlation for internal-to-auth-server spray patterns.
7) HUNT: Search for any successful authentications from the 17 attacker IPs in the attack windows and 48h after; check for lateral movement, new SSH keys, or persistence mechanisms on any account that may have been compromised.
8) LONG-TERM: Implement credential leak monitoring; deploy passwordless auth for service accounts; segment authentication infrastructure.
